mnemonics: Unprotected Critical Racing Undermines eXecution (U.C.R.U.X.) - highlights the core problem and the need for protection (like a locked box) in the critical section.
keyPoints: A race condition occurs when the outcome of execution depends on the particular order in which processes access shared data.
The critical section is the part of the code where shared resources are accessed and must be protected.
Synchronization primitives like mutex locks and semaphores are used to prevent race conditions by ensuring mutual exclusion.
commonMistakes: Assuming that a simple test-and-set (like 'if (file exists)') is sufficient to avoid a race, without atomic operations or proper locking.
Focusing on high-level program logic and forgetting that operations like i++ are not atomic and consist of multiple machine instructions (read, increment, write).
Using synchronization primitives incorrectly, leading to deadlock (e.g., locking mutexes in a different order across processes) or forgetting to unlock a acquired resource.
